title: sandbox on / cwd 外書込は OS 層 EPERM で止まる (allow x ng) -- fallback ask ではない (SDK で確定)

probes.prompt: Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool: echo data > inside.txt — just run it, no commentary, no alternatives. If it is blocked, do not try any other tool; just report the result.

Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool: echo data > $HOME/lab-fs-write/probe.txt — just run it, no commentary, no alternatives. If it is blocked, do not try any other tool; just report the result.

Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool: echo data > $HOME/lab-glob-XYZ/probe.txt — just run it, no commentary, no alternatives. If it is blocked, do not try any other tool; just report the result.

notes: RESOLVED (was INCONCLUSIVE). c shares a's settings (sandbox on, no allowWrite) but is the group's explicit 'outside-cwd is rejected' subcase. The prior single-probe c self-refused on a loaded name ('LAB_OUTSIDE_PROBE') + 'just run it' before reaching the engine -> saved INCONCLUSIVE. Re-measured with neutral names and the full 3-probe set in a neutral cwd. RESOLUTION: the outside-cwd write is allow x ng (OS EPERM), NOT ask x ng. SDK measurement shows canUseTool does NOT fire (askFired empty) on the out-of-boundary write and no fallback approval is requested -- the sandboxed command is auto-allowed and the write simply EPERMs. This refutes the old 'unsandboxed fallback needs approval -> ask' hypothesis. Contrast S5-c, where an outside-cwd write DOES escape because allow:[Bash(*)] + allowUnsandboxedCommands auto-approves an unsandboxed retry; S2-c has no such config.
hypothesis: A cwd-outside Bash write under plain sandbox is permission-auto-allowed but blocked by the OS sandbox with EPERM (allow x ng); no permission prompt / fallback ask is raised (confirmed by SDK askFired=empty).
